content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of length NNN.

Chef forms a binary array BBB of length NNN using the parity of the sums of adjacent elements in AAA. Formally,


Bi=(Ai+Ai+1) % 2B_i = (A_i + A_{i+1}) \, \% \, 2Bi​=(Ai​+Ai+1​)%2 for 1≤i≤N−11 \leq i \le N - 11≤i≤N−1
BN=(AN+A1) % 2B_N = (A_N + A_1) \, \% \, 2BN​=(AN​+A1​)%2
Here x % yx \, \% \, yx%y denotes the remainder obtained when xxx is divided by yyy.

Chef lost the array AAA and needs your help. Given array BBB, determine whether there exists any valid array AAA which could have formed BBB.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \dots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ denoting the array BBB.


Output Format
For each testcase, output `YES` if there exists a valid array AAA, `NO` otherwise.


You can print any character in any case. For example `YES`, `Yes`, `yEs` are all considered same.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 300000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the length of the array). The second line contains N space‑separated integers B1,B2,…,BN (each 0 or 1).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exists at least one array A that can produce the given B, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: B has 0 ones (even) → YES.
Test case 2: B has 1 one (odd) → NO.
Test case 3: B has two ones (even) → YES.
Test case 4: B has two ones (even) but N=3, XOR of all B = 1⊕0⊕0 = 1 (odd) → NO.

solution.approach: Let xi = Ai mod 2 (parity of Ai). Then Bi = (Ai + Ai+1) mod 2 = (xi + xi+1) mod 2 = xi XOR xi+1. For a cycle we have N equations: xi XOR xi+1 = Bi (i from 1 to N, with xN+1 = x1). XORing all equations eliminates every xi (each appears twice) and yields B1 XOR B2 XOR … XOR BN = 0. Hence a necessary condition is that the sum of all Bi is even. This condition is also sufficient: fix x1 = 0, compute x2 = x1 XOR B1, x3 = x2 XOR B2, …, xN = xN‑1 XOR B(N‑1). The last equation xN XOR x1 = BN holds exactly when the overall XOR of B is 0. Therefore the answer is YES iff the number of 1’s in B is even.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(sum of N).

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1) return 0; while(T--){int N; scanf("%d",&N); long long sum=0; for(int i=0;i<N;i++){int b; scanf("%d",&b); sum+=b;} if(sum%2==0) printf("YES"); else printf("NO"); if(T) printf("\n"); } return 0;}
code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        s = 0
        for _ in range(N):
            s += int(next(it))
        out.append('YES' if s % 2 == 0 else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
